title: Determine the exact value of R(B11, B13)
lean_gate_reason: The proof candidate looks short and formalizable, but Lean is not the shortest remaining path to a sealed packet. The remaining bottleneck is publication-facing: bounded novelty confidence, clean exposition of the counting argument, and publication audit rather than formalization overhead.
micro_paper_assessment: Verify-stage assessment: the Goodman/regularity/spectral packet appears correct after a tiny repair, and if later sealed it would still be a micro-paper shaped exact-value note rather than an instance-only curiosity. The remaining uncertainty is bounded prior-art confidence and publication audit, not theorem shape.
family_affinity: strong almost-diagonal book Ramsey corridor; adjacent exact anchor is R(B12, B13) = 51
generalization_signal: Goodman-plus-convexity may transfer to nearby one-gap B_{n-2} versus B_n cases, but the final obstruction here is parameter-specific.
candidate_theorem_slice: Any 49-vertex graph avoiding B11 whose complement avoids B13 must be 24-regular with lambda = 10 and mu = 13, forcing the infeasible strongly regular parameter set (49,24,10,13).
strongest_honest_claim: The current packet contains a faithful and apparently correct proof candidate that every 49-vertex graph contains B11 or its complement contains B13, so R(B11, B13) = 49 if the bounded rediscovery position survives later audit. The honest harness label remains CANDIDATE until Lean is complete.
next_action: Run publication_audit on the exact-value novelty position and paper packet. If that stays clean, decide whether Lean adds enough sealing value to justify the formalization cost.
